content.text:
Advanced Data Visualization
Semester:
3  |
Type:
UEF31  |
VHS:
42h  |
Weekly hours:
3h  |
Lecture:
1h30  |
Lab:
1h30
Personal work VHS:
21h  |
Coefficient:
3  |
Credits:
5
Teaching Objectives
Know how to represent data in an effective and aesthetic way.
Course Content (21h)
Chapter I – Foundations of Data Visualization
Objectives, principles, and issues of visualisation
Data types and associated representation types
Visual perception and chart readability
Design rules (Tufte, Cairo, Few)
Chapter II – Univariate and Bivariate Visualisation
Charts for quantitative variables: histograms, boxplots, density plots
Charts for qualitative variables: bars, pie charts, Pareto diagrams
Scatter plots, trend curves, correlation matrices
Using colours, sizes, and shapes to enrich analysis
Chapter III – Multidimensional Visualisation
Parallel coordinates, scatter plot matrices
t-SNE, UMAP for high-dimensional visualisation
Cluster, group, and class visualisation
Chapter IV – Visualisation Tools with Python
Matplotlib: figure customisation, subplots
Seaborn: enriched statistical visualisation
Plotly: interactive visualisations (zoom, hover, filters)
Altair / Bokeh: declarative and dynamic visualisation
Chapter V – Dashboards and Interactive Visualisation
Dashboard concept: objectives, structure, interactivity
Introduction to Streamlit, Dash (Python)
Data exploration links, filtering, and dynamic updates
Practical case: creating a simple dashboard
Chapter VI – Geospatial Visualisation
Geographic and cartographic data
Static and interactive maps: Folium, GeoPandas
Spatial data visualisation on choropleth maps
Case study: regional, logistical, or urban data
Chapter VII – Best Practices, Design, and Storytelling
Choosing the right chart for the data type
Common mistakes to avoid (truncated axes, overload, etc.)
Visualisation for persuasion: narration, data staging
Real examples of data storytelling (journalism, marketing, sciences)
Assessment
Final examination (in person): 60%
Continuous assessment (CC): 40% — written tests, personal work
Bibliography
Edward Tufte (2001).
The Visual Display of Quantitative Information
. Graphics Press.
Alberto Cairo (2016).
The Truthful Art: Data, Charts, and Maps for Communication
. New Riders.
Stephen Few (2012).
Show Me the Numbers: Designing Tables and Graphs to Enlighten
. Analytics Press.
Colin Ware (2020).
Information Visualization: Perception for Design
. Morgan Kaufmann.
